name: Memory: does it remember me?
tagline: You told the AI something last week, and today it acts like you've never met — then a different AI greets you like an old friend and it's faintly unnerving. Both are explainable, and neither is magic. Forgetting is the default; remembering is a feature; and a good one is a notebook you can open.
overview: The module that kills two confusions at once: "I told it last week, why doesn't it remember?" and its uneasy twin, "why did that other one seem to remember me?" Build on the desk from the previous module: your context is what's on the desk right now, and each new conversation starts with a CLEAN desk — swept bare — which is exactly why the AI "forgot." Remembering across conversations needs something extra: a notebook kept beside the desk. During your chats, important things — your name, your preferences, decisions you made — get written into the notebook, and at the start of each new conversation the relevant notes are placed back on the desk. So when an AI does remember you, it isn't reading your mind or watching you; it's retrieving saved notes. Two kinds, in plain words: SHORT-TERM memory is the desk itself, this conversation only; LONG-TERM memory is the notebook, which persists across conversations. Keep one more line straight, because the next module depends on it: memory is what the AI learned about YOU from your chats, while your DOCUMENTS — next module — are the material you deliberately handed it. Notebook versus binder. The module closes on the control question every beginner should ask of any AI product that remembers: can I SEE the notebook, EDIT it, and DELETE from it? Good tools say yes, and that's the standard to demand. Nothing here is technical — it's the difference between a swept desk and a kept notebook, and the reassurance that a notebook you can open is not a thing to fear.
objectives: Explain why an AI forgets by default — each new conversation starts with a clean desk (this is the context window from the previous module) — and why remembering across conversations needs something extra: a notebook kept beside the desk that captures notes from your chats and puts the relevant ones back on the desk next time.

Tell short-term memory apart from long-term memory in one plain line each — short-term is the desk, this conversation only; long-term is the notebook, saved across conversations — and keep memory-about-you separate from your uploaded documents (notebook versus binder, the binder being the next module).

State the control question to ask of any AI that remembers you — can I see it, edit it, and delete from it? — and understand why a memory you can open and manage is reassuring rather than creepy: it's retrieval of saved notes, not surveillance.

lessons.title: Why it forgot — the swept desk and the notebook beside it
lessons.objective: Understand why an AI forgets by default (a clean desk each conversation) and how remembering works when it happens — a notebook beside the desk that captures notes from your chats and restores the relevant ones next time; tell short-term from long-term memory.
lessons.body:
## The complaint, out loud

You had a long, useful conversation with an AI last week. You told it your name, the project you're working on, how you like things phrased. Today you come back, pick up where you left off — and it has no idea who you are. Blank. As if the whole thing never happened. It's genuinely annoying, and it feels almost rude.

So here's the plain-English reason, and once you have it, the annoyance turns into something you can actually work with.

## The desk gets swept between conversations

In the previous module you met the AI's **desk** — the space that holds everything it can see and think about in the conversation you're having right now. The plain name for that desk, the term of art, is the **context window**: the AI's short-term working space for this chat. Whatever's on the desk, it can use. Whatever isn't, it can't.

Here's the part nobody tells you. **When a conversation ends, the desk gets swept completely clean.** Start a new chat and you're not sitting back down at last week's desk with all your papers still spread out — you're at a *fresh, empty desk*, swept bare. Everything you'd laid out before is gone. That's not a fault or a bug. It's the default, and it's the entire reason the AI "forgot" you: there was never anything wrong with its memory, because by default, across conversations, **there is no memory at all.** Each conversation is its own clean desk, and the last one was cleared away.

Sit with that for a second, because it flips the question. The surprise isn't that the AI forgot last week. Given a swept desk, forgetting is the *only* thing that could happen. The real question is the opposite one: when an AI *does* remember you across conversations — and some do — **how on earth is it doing that?**

## The notebook beside the desk

Here's how. Remembering across conversations needs something the desk alone can't provide: a **notebook kept right beside the desk.**

Picture it literally. While you're chatting — while things are spread out on the desk — a few important items get **copied into a notebook** that sits next to it: your name, the fact that you prefer short replies, a decision you made about your project, a correction you gave. The notebook isn't swept when the conversation ends. It stays. And at the **start of your next conversation**, before you've even said much, the relevant notes get pulled from the notebook and **laid back onto the fresh desk**. So the new conversation doesn't begin truly empty — it begins with the right pages from last time already set out in front of the AI.

That is the whole mechanism. Notes captured from your chats, kept in a notebook that survives, and placed back on the desk when they're relevant. The plain word for this notebook — the term you'll see on AI products — is **memory**. When a tool advertises that it "remembers you," this notebook is what it means: **not magic, not mind-reading, not watching you** — just saved notes, retrieved.

And that reframes the uneasy version of the question too. If you've ever had an AI greet you by name or recall a preference and felt a small chill — *how does it know that?* — this is the answer, and it's deflating in the best way. It knows because, in an earlier chat, that fact got written into the notebook, and now it's been placed back on the desk. It isn't surveillance. It's retrieval.

## Two kinds of memory, one line each

Now you can name the two kinds cleanly, because you've already met both.

- **Short-term memory is the desk itself.** It holds this conversation, and only this conversation. It's excellent while you're sitting there — everything you've said in this chat is right in front of the AI — and it's wiped when you get up. The desk from the previous module *is* short-term memory. (Its technical name, again, is the context window.)
- **Long-term memory is the notebook.** It holds the handful of things worth keeping, and it **persists across conversations.** It's how anything survives from one chat to the next.

One sentence to carry out of here: **the desk is short-term and gets swept every conversation; the notebook is long-term and stays.** If a fact needs to outlive today's chat, it has to make it into the notebook — the desk alone will never carry it to tomorrow. That single distinction explains every "why did it forget / why did it remember" moment you'll ever have with an AI. The next lesson is about the one thing that separates a notebook you should trust from one you shouldn't: whether you're allowed to open it.

knowledgeCheck.stem: You had a detailed chat with an AI last week; today, in a new conversation, it doesn't remember any of it. Why — in plain terms?

options.text: Each new conversation starts at a fresh, swept-clean desk — by default there's no memory across chats, so last week's desk was cleared away
The AI is malfunctioning; a working AI would automatically remember every past conversation
It's deliberately ignoring you or pretending not to know you
It remembers everything but is choosing to hide that it does

knowledgeCheck.explanation: The desk — the context window — holds only the current conversation and is swept clean when the chat ends, so by default nothing carries across conversations; that's why last week is gone. It isn't a malfunction (b) — forgetting across chats is the normal default, not a broken feature, and remembering is the thing that takes something extra. It isn't ignoring or pretending (c): there's genuinely nothing there to recall unless it was saved. And it isn't secretly holding your history back (d) — without a memory feature, that history simply wasn't kept.

knowledgeCheck.stem: When an AI does remember you across conversations, what is actually happening — the notebook-beside-the-desk idea?

options.text: Important notes from your earlier chats were saved in a notebook that isn't swept, and the relevant ones are placed back on the desk at the start of a new conversation
It is reading your mind or watching everything you do to know things about you
The desk is never actually cleared, so every past conversation is always sitting there in full
It genuinely knew you all along, the way a person who's met you would

knowledgeCheck.explanation: Memory is a notebook kept beside the desk: a few important things get written down during your chats, the notebook survives when the desk is swept, and the relevant notes are laid back on the fresh desk next time — saved notes, retrieved. It's not mind-reading or surveillance (b); it's ordinary retrieval of things that were captured in earlier conversations. The desk really is cleared each conversation (c) — the notebook, not the desk, is what persists. And it didn't know you all along (d): it knows only what made it into the notebook.

lessons.title: Notebook versus binder — and the one question to ask any AI that remembers you
lessons.objective: Separate memory (what the AI learned about you) from your uploaded documents (what you handed it) — notebook versus binder — and adopt the control test: can I see, edit, and delete the memory? Closing with how this shows up in Automatos.
lessons.body:
## Two things people mix up: the notebook and the binder

There's a distinction that trips up almost everyone, and getting it straight now will save you confusion in the very next module. It's the difference between what the AI **learned about you** and what you **deliberately handed it.**

Think of two objects on your desk.

- The **notebook** is **memory** — the thing this whole module is about. It fills up *on its own, from your conversations*: your name, your preferences, decisions and corrections you made along the way. You didn't sit down and file these; they got jotted down as you chatted. It's the AI's growing sense of *you*.
- The **binder** is your **documents** — material you *chose* to hand over: a report you uploaded, a price list, a policy, a contract. You put those there on purpose, as reference for a task. The binder is the subject of the next module (teaching the AI your stuff, the thing called RAG); flag it here only so you never confuse the two.

The clean way to hold it: **the notebook is what the AI picked up about you; the binder is what you gave it to work from.** Memory versus documents. They feel similar — both are "stuff the AI knows that isn't in this chat" — but they arrive completely differently. One accumulates quietly from talking to you; the other you deliberately place on the desk. Keep them apart and the next module lands cleanly.

## The one question to ask any AI that remembers you

Now the part that actually matters for you as a person using these tools. A notebook full of things an AI has quietly noted about you *should* raise an eyebrow — not because the mechanism is sinister (it's just saved notes), but because a notebook you **can't see** is a fair thing to be wary of. So there's a single test that separates a trustworthy memory from one to avoid, and it's three plain questions:

1. **Can I see it?** Can you open the notebook and read what the AI has stored about you — actually look at the list?
2. **Can I edit it?** If a note is wrong — a mistaken preference, an outdated fact — can you correct it?
3. **Can I delete from it?** If you don't want something remembered, can you strike it out permanently, so it's gone for good?

**A good tool answers yes to all three.** The notebook is yours; you're allowed to open it, fix it, and tear pages out. A tool that keeps a notebook on you but won't let you look inside is the one to be cautious of — and now you know exactly what to ask for by name: *show me the memory, let me edit it, let me delete it.*

This is also the cure for the creepiness worry, and it's worth saying plainly. What makes memory feel unsettling is the sense that something is being kept about you in the dark. Take away the dark — let you open the notebook, read every line, change what's wrong, and delete what you don't want — and the unease drains out of it. A memory you can inspect and control isn't surveillance; it's a convenience that stays on your terms. **The standard to demand is simple: if it remembers you, you get to open the notebook.**

## The whole module in three lines

- The desk gets swept each conversation (short-term memory), so forgetting is the default.
- The notebook persists (long-term memory), so remembering is a *feature* — saved notes, retrieved — not magic and not surveillance.
- A notebook is what the AI learned about *you*; a binder is the documents *you* handed it (next module) — and a trustworthy notebook is one you can see, edit, and delete from.

## The Automatos peek

Automatos agents keep exactly this notebook. There's a short-term memory for the conversation you're in and a long-term one that persists across conversations, and the platform's own documentation describes agents automatically storing the important things from your chats — key decisions, your preferences, corrections you made — so context builds up over time instead of vanishing each session. Crucially, it passes the control test: there's a Memory tab where you can browse what's been stored, search it in plain language, edit a memory that's wrong, and permanently delete anything you don't want kept (the docs note deletion is permanent — the agent will no longer recall it). The notebook is real, and it's yours to open.

knowledgeCheck.stem: What's the difference between an AI's memory and the documents you upload to it — the notebook versus the binder?

options.text: Memory is what the AI picked up about you from your conversations (the notebook); documents are material you deliberately handed it to work from (the binder)
They're the same thing — memory and uploaded documents are just two names for one feature
Memory is the files you upload, while documents are the things the AI quietly noticed about you
Memory is more reliable than documents because the AI created it itself rather than being given it

knowledgeCheck.explanation: The notebook (memory) fills up on its own from your chats — your name, preferences, decisions — while the binder (documents) is material you chose to hand over, like a report or price list; they arrive completely differently. They're not one feature under two names (b): one accumulates from talking, the other you place deliberately. Option (c) has the two swapped — memory is what the AI noted about you, documents are the files you uploaded. And "self-created memory is more reliable" (d) is a false claim about accuracy; the distinction is about where each comes from, not which to trust more.

knowledgeCheck.stem: An AI product tells you it remembers things about you. What's the single most useful test of whether its memory is trustworthy?

options.text: Whether you can see what it stored, edit anything that's wrong, and permanently delete what you don't want kept
Whether the memory feature sounds advanced and impressive in the marketing
Whether it remembers as much as possible about you, since more stored means better
Nothing — if an AI keeps a memory about you, there's no way to know or control what's in it

knowledgeCheck.explanation: The control test is exactly the three questions — can I see it, edit it, and delete from it? — and a trustworthy tool answers yes to all three, because the notebook is yours to open and manage. Impressive-sounding marketing (b) tells you nothing about whether you can actually inspect and control the notebook. "More stored is better" (c) is backwards — a large notebook you can't see or delete from is worse, not better; control matters more than volume. And it's simply untrue that you can't know or control it (d) — good tools let you view, edit, and delete, and that's the standard to demand.

questions.stem: Why does an AI forget everything from a previous conversation by default?

options.text: Each new conversation starts at a fresh, swept-clean desk — by default nothing carries across chats
It has a poor memory that better AIs don't have
It saves everything permanently but refuses to show it to you
The internet connection resets and wipes the stored history each time

questions.explanation: The desk (the context window) holds only the current conversation and is cleared when the chat ends, so across conversations there's no memory unless something extra keeps it — forgetting is the default. It isn't that some AIs simply have "better memory" (b) — remembering across chats takes an added feature, a notebook, not a superior model. It isn't secretly hoarding your history (c) — without a memory feature, nothing was kept. And it has nothing to do with a connection resetting (d); it's simply that the working desk is swept between conversations.

questions.stem: In this module's analogy, what is "memory" — the thing that lets an AI remember you across conversations?

options.text: A notebook kept beside the desk: notes captured from your chats that persist, and get placed back on the desk when relevant
The desk itself, which quietly keeps all your past conversations laid out
A live feed that watches everything you do so the AI always knows about you
A guarantee that the AI understands you the way a person who's known you for years would

questions.explanation: Memory is the notebook beside the desk — a few important things get written down during your chats, the notebook survives when the desk is swept, and the relevant notes are restored to the desk next time. It isn't the desk (b): the desk is short-term and gets cleared every conversation, which is precisely why a separate, persistent notebook is needed. It isn't a surveillance feed (c) — it's retrieval of notes saved from your own conversations, not watching. And it's not deep human-style understanding (d); it's saved notes, nothing more mystical than that.

questions.stem: How do short-term memory and long-term memory map onto the desk-and-notebook picture?

options.text: Short-term memory is the desk (this conversation only, swept each time); long-term memory is the notebook (persists across conversations)
Short-term is the notebook that persists; long-term is the desk that's wiped each chat
They're the same thing — the two names describe one identical store
Short-term means fast answers and long-term means slow answers

questions.explanation: Short-term memory is the desk — it holds the current conversation and is swept clean each time — while long-term memory is the notebook that survives and carries things from one chat to the next. Option (b) reverses them: the persistent thing is the notebook (long-term), and the wiped thing is the desk (short-term). They're not one identical store (c) — the whole point is that one is cleared each conversation and the other persists. And the labels are about how long things are kept, not the speed of replies (d).

questions.stem: A friend says: "AI memory is creepy — it's basically spying on me." Based on this module, what's the accurate, calming correction?

options.text: It's retrieval of notes saved from your own conversations, not surveillance — and in a trustworthy tool you can see, edit, and delete those notes
They're right — memory means the AI is secretly monitoring everything you do everywhere
It's fine because the AI forgets it all instantly anyway, so nothing is ever stored
There's no way to know what's stored, so the only safe move is to never use any AI

questions.explanation: The mechanism is saved notes from your own chats, retrieved when relevant — not spying — and the cure for the unease is control: a good tool lets you see the notebook, correct it, and delete from it. It isn't secret monitoring of everything you do (b): it only captures things from your conversations with it, and you can inspect them. It's not that nothing is stored (c) — long-term memory genuinely persists; that's the point of it. And "never use any AI" (d) overshoots — the sensible response is to demand a memory you can view, edit, and delete, not to avoid the tools.

questions.stem: You want a fact you mentioned once — say, that you prefer replies in British English — to still apply in next month's conversations. What has to happen?

options.text: That preference has to make it into the notebook (long-term memory); the desk alone won't carry it past today's conversation
Nothing — the desk keeps everything you've ever said available forever
You need a more powerful AI, because memory is purely a matter of model strength
It's impossible for any AI to apply a preference across separate conversations

questions.explanation: For a fact to outlive today's chat it must be written into the notebook — long-term memory — because the desk (short-term) is swept every conversation and can't carry anything to next month on its own. The desk does not keep everything forever (b): it's cleared when the conversation ends, which is the core idea of the module. It's not about raw model power (c) — persistence comes from having a memory feature, a notebook, not from a stronger model. And it's very much possible (d): that's exactly what long-term memory is for.

questions.stem: You upload your company handbook so the AI can answer questions from it, and separately the AI has noted that you like concise answers. Which is the "binder" and which is the "notebook"?

options.text: The handbook is the binder (documents you handed it); the concise-answers preference is the notebook (memory it picked up about you)
The handbook is the notebook and the preference is the binder
Both are the binder, because anything the AI knows counts as a document
Both are the notebook, because everything the AI holds is memory

questions.explanation: The handbook is material you deliberately handed over as reference — the binder (documents, the next module's topic) — while the preference for concise answers is something the AI picked up about you from your chats — the notebook (memory). Option (b) swaps them. Not everything is the binder (c): a preference you never filed as a document is memory, not an uploaded file. And not everything is the notebook (d): a handbook you chose to upload is a document you supplied, not something the AI quietly learned about you. The two arrive differently, and keeping them apart is what sets up the next module.

questions.stem: Which three questions make up the control test for any AI product that remembers you?

options.text: Can I see what's stored, can I edit what's wrong, and can I permanently delete what I don't want kept?
Is it fast, is it cheap, and does it have a nice interface?
Which company made it, how big is it, and is it the most popular?
Does it remember as much as possible, and does it do so without ever asking me?

questions.explanation: The control test is see, edit, delete — and a trustworthy tool answers yes to all three, because a notebook kept about you should be yours to open and manage. Speed, cost, and looks (b) are ordinary product questions that say nothing about whether you can inspect and control the memory. Vendor, size, and popularity (c) don't tell you whether you can view or delete what's stored. And option (d) is the opposite of the standard: maximising what's remembered while removing your say is exactly the pattern to be wary of.

questions.stem: What's the one-line takeaway that ties this whole module together?

options.text: The desk gets swept (short-term); the notebook persists (long-term) — forgetting is the default, remembering is a feature, and a good notebook is one you can open
AI has a perfect photographic memory of every conversation you've ever had with it
AI can never remember anything across conversations under any circumstances
Memory and uploaded documents are the same thing, so there's nothing to keep separate

questions.explanation: The takeaway is precisely that: the desk is swept each conversation (short-term), the notebook persists (long-term), so forgetting is the default and remembering is an added feature — and a trustworthy notebook is one you can see, edit, and delete from. It's not a perfect photographic memory (b): only a few important notes are kept, and only if a memory feature exists. It's not "never remembers" either (c) — with a notebook, it can and does persist. And memory is not the same as documents (d) — notebook versus binder is a distinction worth keeping, and it sets up the next module.

resources.title: Automatos AI — Memory (explore and manage what your agents remember)
resources.annotation: The grounding for this module's peek. It describes the two tiers in the platform's own words — a short-term memory for recent conversation and active task state, and a long-term memory that persists across sessions for important facts, decisions, and preferences — which is exactly the desk-and-notebook picture. It also documents the control test directly: a Memory tab to browse and search stored memories, plus edit, delete (noted as permanent), and pin. Read it as proof that "a notebook you can open" is a real, managed thing, not a slogan.

resources.title: Google — Generative AI Leader exam guide (Fundamentals of generative AI)
resources.annotation: A vendor-light, business-toned primer on what generative AI is and how it behaves. For this module, use it to reinforce the plain foundation the analogy sits on — that a model works from what's in front of it in a conversation, which is why anything meant to persist beyond that conversation needs to be stored and supplied again. Beginner-friendly and non-technical; read only the fundamentals section, and take no dated capability claim from it as fixed.

resources.title: OpenAI — a plain explainer on assistant memory (how a consumer AI remembers you)
resources.annotation: A general-audience help page describing how a mainstream consumer AI's memory works: it can remember things across conversations, you can see what it has stored, and you can edit or delete those memories (or turn the feature off). Used here purely to show the module's control test — see, edit, delete — is a real expectation of good tools in the wild, named vendor-neutrally as one example among several. Orientation only; specific behaviours and settings change over time, so treat the durable idea (you should be able to view and delete), not any exact menu, as the takeaway.

videos.title: 08 · Why it forgot — the swept desk and the notebook beside it, in 7 minutes
08 · Deep Dive — short-term vs long-term, notebook vs binder, and the control test
